content.text: How can I change the name of a function used as a command in my API while keeping its original definition intact?

This decorator works by utilizing Python's higher-order functions to wrap your targeted function. The wrapper then uses setattr to assign the new name directly onto the function object.

```python
def named(new_name):
    """
    Sets given string as command name instead of the function name.
    The string is used verbatim without further processing.

    Usage::

        @named('load')
        def do_load_some_stuff_and_keep_the_original_function_name(args):
            ...

    The resulting command will be available only as ``load``.  To add aliases
    without renaming the command, check :func:`aliases`.

    .. versionadded:: 0.19
    """
    def wrapper(func):
        setattr(func, ATTR_NAME, new_name)
        return func
    return wrapper
```

The implementation relies entirely on setting a specific attribute, `ATTR_NAME`, which must be recognized and used by the system that calls this decorator. This means the function's internal name remains unchanged even if its exposed command name is different.
If I use this decorated function as part of a class method, will it still apply the renaming to the class instance or just the unbound method?

This approach generally works best for standalone functions due to how attribute modification interacts with bound methods. For classes, you would need a different implementation leveraging `__call__` within the class context.
That makes sense; I'll stick to using it only on top-level functions then.
